claim: Binding rent control (price ceilings below market clearing with enforcement teeth) reduces aggregate housing supply in affected markets, reduces the quality of existing rent-controlled stock through reduced maintenance incentives, and produces distributional effects favouring long-tenure incumbents over mobile workers and new household formation. These effects are well-identified in the literature and represent a framework-validation opportunity: consensus among economists is ~93% that rent control reduces housing availability and quality (IGM Forum survey). If the framework cannot reproduce this on natural- experiment data (Berlin Mietendeckel 2020-2021, San Francisco 1994 expansion, Stockholm persistent control), it is broken.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Rent control in San Francisco, Berlin, and Stockholm produces measurable reductions in rental supply, unit quality, and turnover — the standard Friedman prediction from 'Roofs or Ceilings?' (1946).
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Audit flagged as FAIL — claim scope period [1946, 2023] anchors on Friedman's 1946 prediction while citing contemporary cities; hypothesis period is [2000, 2023]. Phase 4C: candidate for rewrite to drop the 1946 framing or relink to a Friedman-theoretical-prediction hypothesis.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Rent control and price controls produce shortages and misallocation.
Rent control at the right design can improve tenure security without substantial supply penalty.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Democratic-socialist position defends second-generation rent stabilisation; framework predicts supply effects dominate.

claim: UK Town and Country Planning Act 1947 and subsequent restrictive-zoning regimes produce measurable housing supply elasticity reductions and price level increases relative to more-permissive planning systems.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: UK Town and Country Planning Act 1947 and subsequent restrictive-zoning regimes produce measurable housing supply elasticity reductions and price level increases relative to more-permissive planning systems.

claim: Vienna's sustained social-housing programme has delivered lower rent burdens and housing-cost inflation than comparable European capitals without supply collapse, a counter-example to the rent-control-supply-destruction narrative.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Vienna's sustained social-housing programme has delivered lower rent burdens and housing-cost inflation than comparable European capitals without supply collapse, a counter-example to the rent-control-supply-destruction narrative.
